This page provides a comprehensive introduction to the go-livepeer repository, which implements the Livepeer protocol - a decentralized video streaming network with crypto-token incentives. The go-livepeer codebase enables running various node types that form the Livepeer network ecosystem.

Purpose and Scope

Go-livepeer implements a live video-streaming network protocol that is fully decentralized, highly scalable, and crypto-token incentivized. The scope of this repository includes:

  • Implementation of all Livepeer network node types (Broadcaster/Gateway, Orchestrator, Transcoder, Redeemer, AI Worker)
  • Video ingest via RTMP and HTTP push protocols
  • Video segmentation, transcoding, and delivery
  • Probabilistic micropayment system for compensating network participants
  • Ethereum blockchain integration for staking, rewards, and payments
  • AI-based media processing capabilities

For information about node deployment strategies, see Multi Orchestrator Setup For GPU transcoding details, see GPU Support

Sources: README.md13-18 core/livepeernode.go1-9

Node Types and Architecture

The Livepeer network consists of several node types, each with specific responsibilities:

  1. Broadcaster/Gateway Node: Acts as the entry point for video streams, handling video ingest, segmentation, and distribution to Orchestrators. Also serves transcoded content to viewers via HLS.

  2. Orchestrator Node: Coordinates the transcoding process, receiving segmented video from Broadcasters, selecting appropriate Transcoders, verifying results, and managing payments.

  3. Transcoder Node: Performs the actual video transcoding work, converting video segments to different formats and resolutions.

  4. Redeemer Node: Specialized service that handles redeeming winning payment tickets on the Ethereum blockchain.

  5. AI Worker Node: Performs AI-based media processing, extending Livepeer's capabilities beyond traditional video transcoding.

Sources: core/livepeernode.go41-59 server/mediaserver.go109-132

Core Components

The go-livepeer codebase is organized around several key components:

LivepeerNode

The LivepeerNode struct defined in core/livepeernode.go112-165 is the central component that can operate as different node types based on configuration. It contains fields and methods common to all node types, as well as type-specific components.

type LivepeerNode struct {
    // Common fields
    Eth      eth.LivepeerEthClient
    WorkDir  string
    NodeType NodeType
    Database *common.DB
    
    // Node-specific fields
    Sender              pm.Sender          // For Broadcaster
    Transcoder          Transcoder         // For Transcoder
    TranscoderManager   *RemoteTranscoderManager // For Orchestrator
    OrchestratorPool    common.OrchestratorPool  // For Broadcaster
    // ... other fields
}

LivepeerServer

The LivepeerServer struct in server/mediaserver.go109-132 handles media server functionality, including:

  • RTMP ingest for live streaming
  • HTTP push ingest for segment-based streaming
  • HLS delivery for playback
  • Playlist management
  • Segment processing and transcoding

BroadcastSessionsManager

The BroadcastSessionsManager in server/broadcast.go490-560 manages the relationship between Broadcasters and Orchestrators:

  • Selects appropriate Orchestrators for transcoding
  • Maintains pools of trusted and untrusted Orchestrators
  • Handles verification of transcoded results
  • Manages session lifecycle with Orchestrators

Sources: server/mediaserver.go156-210 server/broadcast.go490-560 core/livepeernode.go112-165

Video Processing Pipeline

The video processing pipeline in go-livepeer involves several stages:

  1. Ingest: Video is ingested via RTMP (gotRTMPStreamHandler) or HTTP push (HandlePush) in server/mediaserver.go395-459

  2. Segmentation: The continuous video stream is segmented into discrete chunks using the RTMP segmenter or processed directly from HTTP push

  3. Session Management: The BroadcastSessionsManager selects appropriate Orchestrators using a selection algorithm based on price, performance, and reputation

  4. Transcoding: Selected Orchestrators receive video segments along with payment tickets, then assign transcoding work to Transcoders

  5. Verification: Orchestrators optionally verify transcoded results using fast verification techniques to ensure quality and accuracy

  6. Delivery: Transcoded segments are returned to the Broadcaster, inserted into HLS playlists, and made available for playback

Sources: server/mediaserver.go395-459 server/broadcast.go608-786 server/mediaserver.go613-664

Probabilistic Micropayment System

Go-livepeer implements a probabilistic micropayment system to enable efficient off-chain payments with minimal on-chain transactions:

  1. Ticket Creation: The Broadcaster creates payment tickets with a low probability of winning but high face value

  2. Ticket Submission: Tickets are attached to video segments and sent to Orchestrators

  3. Ticket Validation: Orchestrators validate tickets and identify winners

  4. Ticket Redemption: Winning tickets (a small percentage) are sent to the Redeemer service, which redeems them on-chain

  5. Max Float Management: The system manages "max float" values, which represent the maximum amount an Orchestrator can claim from a Broadcaster

This approach allows for many "payment attempts" to be made off-chain, with only winning tickets requiring on-chain processing, dramatically reducing gas costs and increasing scalability.

Sources: server/broadcast.go52-56 doc/redeemer.md

Ethereum Integration

Go-livepeer integrates with the Ethereum blockchain for various on-chain operations:

  1. Node Registration: Orchestrators register on-chain with their service URI and price information

  2. Staking: Orchestrators stake LPT tokens as security and to participate in the network

  3. Payments: The TicketBroker contract handles payment redemption from Broadcasters to Orchestrators

  4. Rewards: Participants can claim LPT rewards through the reward service

  5. Round Management: The round initialization service manages protocol rounds

The Ethereum integration is configurable through various flags in cmd/livepeer/livepeer.go177-214 including:

  • -ethUrl: Ethereum JSON-RPC URL
  • -ethController: Protocol smart contract address
  • -ethKeystorePath: Path to ETH keystore directory
  • -maxGasPrice: Maximum gas price for transactions

Sources: cmd/livepeer/livepeer.go177-214 doc/ethereum.md

Configuration and Deployment

Go-livepeer uses a flag-based configuration system defined in cmd/livepeer/livepeer.go112-244 Key flags include:

Flag CategoryExample FlagsDescription
Node Type-gateway, -orchestrator, -transcoder, -redeemer, -aiWorkerDetermine node role
Network-rtmpAddr, -httpAddr, -cliAddrNetwork binding addresses
Transcoding-transcodingOptions, -maxSessions, -nvidiaConfigure transcoding options
Ethereum-ethUrl, -ethController, -maxGasPriceEthereum connection settings
Pricing-pricePerUnit, -pixelsPerUnitSet transcoding price
Authentication-authWebhookUrlAuthentication webhook

The node can be started with the appropriate combination of flags based on the desired node type and configuration.

Conclusion

Go-livepeer implements a comprehensive decentralized video processing network with specialized node types working together to provide transcoding services. This overview has covered the main components, architectural patterns, and key systems. Additional details on specific aspects can be found in the other wiki pages.

Sources: cmd/livepeer/livepeer.go26-110 README.md
